name: Homeostatic Reinforcement Learning (HRL) with Drive-Reduction Reward
description: Algebraic drive-reduction framework integrated with tabular Q-learning. The agent maintains a scalar energy level that decays over time and is restored by eating food. Drive is defined as the convex distance between current energy and a setpoint; reward is defined as the reduction in drive produced by each action. A Q-table maps discretised (position, energy-bin) states to action values, updated via temporal-difference learning. Actions are selected by softmax over Q-values. Grounded in Keramati & Gutkin (2014) who proved that reward-maximisation and homeostatic stability are mathematically equivalent objectives under temporal discounting.

variables.name: energy_level
variables.description: Scalar physiological variable representing the agent's current energy/nutrient store, normalised to [0,1]

variables.name: setpoint
variables.description: Ideal target value for the energy level; the equilibrium the agent seeks to maintain

variables.name: drive
variables.description: Scalar motivational signal measuring absolute deviation from setpoint, raised to power n. Zero at setpoint, increases monotonically with deviation.

variables.name: reward
variables.description: Drive reduction produced by the outcome at time t; positive when drive decreases (beneficial), negative when drive increases (harmful)

variables.name: action_value_table
variables.description: Q-table mapping discretised state s (position + energy bin) to expected discounted sum of future drive-reductions for each action

variables.name: td_error
variables.description: Temporal-difference reward prediction error; drives Q-value updates

variables.name: external_state
variables.description: Discretised external state: tuple of (x, y, energy_bin) where energy_bin is a quantised version of h(t)

parameters.name: drive_exponent
parameters.source: Keramati & Gutkin (2014), quadratic drive convexity ensures smooth gradient and accelerating urgency

parameters.name: energy_decay_rate
parameters.source: Calibrated; represents metabolic cost per time step

parameters.name: energy_gain_from_eating
parameters.source: Calibrated; energy restored per food item consumed

parameters.name: movement_energy_cost
parameters.source: Calibrated; extra metabolic cost per move action

parameters.name: discount_factor
parameters.source: Keramati & Gutkin (2014), required strictly < 1 for homeostatic optimality

parameters.name: energy_discretisation_bins
parameters.source: Calibrated; discretises [0,1] into 10 bins for Q-table state

rules.description: Energy dynamics: energy decays each step by c_dec, is further reduced by c_move if the agent moved, and is increased by c_eat if the agent ate food. Clipped to [0,1].
rules.pseudocode:
moved = 1 if action in ['move_up','move_down','move_left','move_right'] else 0
ate = 1 if (action == 'eat' and last_action_result.get('consumed', False)) else 0
h = clip(h - c_dec - c_move * moved + c_eat * ate, 0.0, 1.0)

rules.description: Drive function: convex deviation of energy from setpoint, raised to exponent n. Symmetric — both deficit and excess generate drive.
Primary reward as drive reduction: the difference between drive before and after the action. Positive reward means the action reduced the drive.
rules.pseudocode:
D_old = abs(h_old - h_star) ** n
D_new = abs(h_new - h_star) ** n
r = D_old - D_new

rules.description: Temporal-difference error: standard Q-learning TD(0) error using drive-reduction reward.
rules.pseudocode: delta = r + gamma * max(Q[s_new, a_] for a_ in all_actions) - Q[s_old, a_taken]

rules.description: Q-value update: standard tabular Q-learning update rule.
rules.pseudocode: Q[s_old, a_taken] = Q[s_old, a_taken] + alpha * delta

rules.description: Softmax action selection: actions are chosen stochastically with probability proportional to exponentiated Q-values, controlled by inverse temperature beta.
rules.pseudocode:
logits = [beta * Q[s, a] for a in available_actions]
probs = softmax(logits)
action = sample_from(available_actions, probs)

decision_logic.description: The agent discretises its state, computes drive, selects actions via softmax over Q-values, and updates its Q-table using drive-reduction reward after each step.
pseudocode: food_here = any(f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'] for f in perception['resources'].get('food', []))
logits = [beta * Q.get((s, a), 0.0) for a in available_actions]
exp_logits = [exp(l - max_logit) for l in logits]  # numerically stable
probs = [e / sum_exp for e in exp_logits]

perception_to_variables.food_at_current_cell: any(f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'] for f in perception['resources'].get('food', []))
perception_to_variables.ate_food: last_action_result.get('consumed', False) if last action was 'eat' else False

env_mapping.reward_source: Internal drive-reduction reward r = D(t) - D(t+1), computed from R3. The env reward from ConsumeEffect (1.0) is NOT used; the agent uses its own homeostatic reward signal.

expected_behaviors.description: Hunger increases without eating: energy decays each step, causing drive to increase monotonically when no food is consumed
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 50 steps without food nearby → assert h decreases each step and D increases each step

expected_behaviors.description: Drive-proportional motivation: the agent becomes increasingly likely to move toward food as drive increases (Q-values for food-seeking actions accumulate more reward when drive is high)
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare action distributions at h=0.3 vs h=0.7 → assert foraging probability is higher at h=0.3

expected_behaviors.description: Satiation suppresses eating: when energy is at or above setpoint, eating produces zero or negative reward, so Q-values for eat decline relative to stay
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h=0.9 (above setpoint 0.8) → assert r for eating is negative (drive increases because h moves further from h*)

expected_behaviors.description: Learning improves efficiency: over many episodes, the agent learns shorter paths to food sources, reducing average drive over time
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compare mean drive in steps 1-100 vs steps 901-1000 → assert later drive is lower

expected_behaviors.description: Q-values converge: after sufficient exploration, Q-value updates (delta) decrease in magnitude, indicating policy stabilisation
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: compute mean |delta| over last 100 steps → assert it is smaller than mean |delta| over first 100 steps

references: Keramati, M. & Gutkin, B. (2014). Homeostatic reinforcement learning for integrating reward collection and physiological stability. eLife 3:e04811.
Hull, C.L. (1943). Principles of Behavior. Referenced for drive-reduction theory foundations.
Cannon, W.B. (1929). Organization for physiological homeostasis. Referenced for negative-feedback control origins.
